narration: You approach the workbench where Grist is adjusting a clamp.
response: Grist looks up, wipes their hands on the apron. "New here? Good. Market needs more people who make things instead of just buying them." They gesture at the workbenches. "Tools are free to use. Materials are your problem. You break something, you fix it."

narration: You look at the racks of tools.
response: "Hammer, tongs, mortar — the basics. They'll do for most work." Grist picks up a pair of tongs, snaps them open and shut. "The trick isn't the tools. It's knowing what wants to be combined with what. Some materials take to each other. Some don't. You learn by trying."

"Tiv, down on Rawstock Row. Small, translucent, talks fast. They stock the basics — shimmerite, coppervine, clay, void glass. Tell them Grist sent you, they'll treat you fair." Grist pauses. "Fairer, anyway."

"You hold two materials, you combine them. Simple as that. Some combinations work, most don't. Start with the basics — shimmerite dust and sealed clay make a good foundation. Coppervine and void glass do something interesting too." Grist taps the workbench. "Once you've got intermediates, you can combine those. That's where it gets good."

"Powder. Iridescent. Comes from a dimension where light is thicker than here — more like a liquid. Shimmerite holds light inside its structure. Mix it with the right binding agent and you get materials that glow, or sense, or respond to attention. Good stuff. Tiv sells it."

"Wire drawn from a plant. Conducts like copper, but it remembers shapes — bend it and let go, it slowly goes back. Some people say it's still alive." Grist shrugs. "Wrap it around something rigid and it holds. Good for making frames, lenses, that kind of thing."

"Dense stuff. Keeps its seal until you work it — pressure, heat, mixing. Good binding agent. Takes on properties of whatever you combine it with. Shimmerite dust plus sealed clay gives you shimmer brick — solid, holds light, useful as a housing for other components."

"Glass from a dimension with less light than ours. Absorbs photons at the edges. Look through it and distances get strange." Grist holds up a hand, squinting through an imaginary shard. "Wrap it in coppervine wire and the glass resonates — picks up vibrations from unusual materials. That's a resonance lens. Useful tool around here."

"Shimmer brick housing, resonance lens inside. Point it at something and the lens darkens if the material is unusual — the stranger the stuff, the darker the glass. Handy for sorting goods, finding quality materials, spotting fakes." Grist looks thoughtful. "Make one and you'll see this market differently."

"Good vendor. Knows materials better than anyone on the Row. Comes from a dimension where everything's transparent — skin, buildings, ground. Makes them good at judging quality. They can see the grain in things." Grist chuckles. "Talks too fast, but that's just how they are."

"Buys finished goods. Oddments Alley, stall near the back. Tall, quiet, very particular about what they'll take. Bring them something well-crafted and unusual and they'll pay fair. Bring them junk and they'll tell you it's junk." Grist nods approvingly. "Honest. I respect that."

narration: You step back from the workbench.
response: Grist nods. "Good luck. Remember — the trick is trying things." They turn back to the forge, adjusting the bellows with one hand.
